coredata.dc:title: Innovative strategy for fabricating nanomaterials to ensure food safety by cold plasma: Principles, mechanisms, and applications

coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Jun-Hu
Lan, Lijuan
Sun, Da-Wen
coredata.dc:description:
Background The utilization of nanomaterials to ensure food safety has been attracting more attention in recent years. The fabrication of nanomaterials is obviously important and also challenging. Conventional nanomaterials fabrication methods including mechanical, hydrothermal, and vapour phase deposition have the disadvantages of high energy consumption, low yield, insufficient control precision, and environmental pollution. Cold plasma as an innovative strategy, comprising ions, electrons, atoms, free radicals, and neutral particles, is very beneficial to fabricate nanomaterials.
Scope and approach This review summarizes the principles and progress of cold plasma-fabricated nanomaterials including inorganic nanomaterials, carbon nanomaterials, framework nanomaterials, and biomass nanomaterials. Various applications of cold plasma-fabricated nanomaterials in food safety for antimicrobial, detection and removal of contaminants, and food packaging are also detailly discussed. The advantages, limitations, and prospects of cold plasma-fabricated nanomaterials for food safety applications are pointed out.
Key findings and conclusions Cold plasma fabrication is environmentally friendly, producing minimal waste and reducing the need for harsh chemicals, aligning with sustainable practices in the food industry. Cold plasma-fabricated nanomaterials have applications in antimicrobial, detection, and removal of pollutant residues, food packaging, and other food safety applications. Future studies should further explore the methodology, safety evaluation, and application of cold plasma preparation of nanomaterials to promote their practical application in the field of food safety.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The relationship between cold plasma, nanomaterials, and food safety applications.
Common cold plasma discharges. (A) corona discharges, (B) glow discharges, (C) dielectric barrier discharges, (D) microplasmas, and (E) plasma jets.

(A) Cold plasma generates reactive oxygen to oxidize metal ions, enabling bottom-up fabrication of metal oxide nanomaterials (Ananth & Mok, 2016); and (B) Top-down fabrication of metal oxide nanoparticles by cold plasma electrolysis using metal electrodes as precursors (Zhu et al., 2021).
(A) Illustration of GO electron reduction by DBD plasma (Kurniawan et al., 2021); and (B) proposed mechanism of the microplasma fabrication of N-doped CDs (Peng et al., 2019).
(A) DBD plasma fabrication framework nanomaterials device diagram; (B) and (C) fabrication of MOFs and COFs by DBD plasma (He et al., 2021; Jiang et al., 2019).

(A) Schematic of cold plasma-fabricated nanomaterials for antimicrobials; (B) cold plasma-fabricated nanomaterials for detection and removal of pollutant residues; and (C) cold plasma-fabricated nanomaterials for food package.
Cold plasma fabrication for nanomaterials to ensure food safety.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The utilization of nanomaterials to ensure food safety has been attracting more attention in recent years. The fabrication of nanomaterials is obviously important and also challenging. Conventional nanomaterials fabrication methods including mechanical, hydrothermal, and vapour phase deposition have the disadvantages of high energy consumption, low yield, insufficient control precision, and environmental pollution. Cold plasma as an innovative strategy, comprising ions, electrons, atoms, free radicals, and neutral particles, is very beneficial to fabricate nanomaterials.

This review summarizes the principles and progress of cold plasma-fabricated nanomaterials including inorganic nanomaterials, carbon nanomaterials, framework nanomaterials, and biomass nanomaterials. Various applications of cold plasma-fabricated nanomaterials in food safety for antimicrobial, detection and removal of contaminants, and food packaging are also detailly discussed. The advantages, limitations, and prospects of cold plasma-fabricated nanomaterials for food safety applications are pointed out.

Cold plasma fabrication is environmentally friendly, producing minimal waste and reducing the need for harsh chemicals, aligning with sustainable practices in the food industry. Cold plasma-fabricated nanomaterials have applications in antimicrobial, detection, and removal of pollutant residues, food packaging, and other food safety applications. Future studies should further explore the methodology, safety evaluation, and application of cold plasma preparation of nanomaterials to promote their practical application in the field of food safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Food safety is related to human health and has always been an issue of concern. Crop cultivation, livestock rearing, food processing, food packaging and transportation, each link is closely related to food safety. Unsafe food can cause diarrhoea, allergies, illness, and even death (Esua et al., 2022a). It is reported that billions of people are at risk from hazardous food (Fung et al., 2018). A sound food safety system can enhance public confidence in food and promote food trade and consumption (Ali et al., 2021b). In addition, food safety is also related to the ecological environment and sustainable development and affects the stability and sustainability of the entire food industry chain (Qin et al., 2021; Ramakrishnan et al., 2021). From the materials perspective, nanomaterials are emerging as an essential way to address food safety issues. Nanomaterials are widely used in microbial detection and antimicrobial agents, contaminant removal, food packaging, and other food safety applications due to their size effect, excellent mechanical and optical properties, chemical reactivity, and antimicrobial properties (Mukhopadhyay et al., 2022; Silvestre et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2021).

Conventional methods for fabricating nanomaterials include mechanical methods, vapour phase deposition, hydrothermal methods, and solution-gel methods (Baig et al., 2021). These traditional methods play an important role in the fabrication of nanomaterials, but there are some limitations including process complexity, low yield, insufficient control precision, high energy consumption, and environmental pollution. Thus, there is an urgent need to develop new environmentally friendly nanomaterial fabrication methods to improve preparation efficiency and reduce costs. Compared to traditional nanomaterial fabrication methods, cold plasma is the emerging efficient, environmentally friendly, controllable, and flexible method for nanomaterial fabrication.

Plasma, consisting of ions, electrons, atoms, free radicals, and combined neutral particles, is considered the fourth state of matter, along with solids, liquids, and gases (Vanraes & Bogaerts, 2018). Plasma can usually be divided into high-temperature plasma and low-temperature plasma (also known as cold plasma) (Chen et al., 2020). Cold plasma is non-thermal plasma, and non-thermal refers to the lack of thermal equilibrium in the discharge environment, where the discharge gas temperature is much lower than the temperature of the excited electrons (Chizoba Ekezie et al., 2017; Zhu, Cheng, Han, & Han, 2023). The non-equilibrium of background gas and excited electrons in cold plasma can effectively control the distribution of chemical products produced by electron collisional excitation (Chiang et al., 2020; Ranjha et al., 2023). In addition, the energy level of the cold plasma is relatively low, thus there are no dramatic thermal effects, which helps minimize energy loss and material damage (Baranov et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2023). The temperature, density, and composition of cold plasma can be adjusted by varying parameters such as gas composition, electric field strength, and radio frequency power. This adjustability makes cold plasma a promising candidate for various applications in nanomaterial fabrication and surface modification (Chen et al., 2015; Zhu et al., 2022). For example, Baranov et al. (2018) reviewed how the functionalization and growth of nanostructures and metamaterials can be achieved in a plasma environment. Jang et al. (2021) reported the mechanism of fabricating polymer films and nanoparticles by atmospheric pressure plasma (APP) under more moderate conditions. These two reviews focus on the mechanism of cold plasma preparation of nanomaterials and lack detailed application scenarios. Also, cold plasma fabrication of nanomaterials for analytical chemistry (He et al., 2022) and biomedical applications (Kaushik et al., 2019) are reviewed. Due to the differences in the applications, the obtained nanomaterials fabricated by cold plasma are correspondingly different. Until now, no related review has been available using cold plasma fabrication of nanomaterials for food safety control.

Cold plasma systems have different discharge schemes according to the plasma state, which can be divided into corona discharge, glow discharge, and dielectric barrier discharge (DBD). According to the applied voltage waveform, it can be divided into direct current (DC), alternating current (AC, at low frequency), radio frequency (RF, at high frequency), microwave discharge, and pulse discharge (Vanraes & Bogaerts, 2018; Esua et al., 2021a). Corona discharge is a localized electrical discharge that typically occurs near a sharp electrode, forming a small plasma region (Obajemihi et al., 2023). Glow discharge is usually low-pressure discharge with low current densities and temperatures (Zhang et al., 2015). DBD is a type of plasma generated by collisional excitation of gas molecules using a high voltage, low current electric field across a medium surface (He et al., 2022). DBD discharge typically has a large volume of plasma discharge area with a wide operating range and is the most common form of cold plasma discharge (Wu et al., 2023). A plasma jet is not strictly a type of plasma discharge, but it has higher local applicability and higher power coupled by blowing out the active particles of the discharge through an auxiliary gas (Scholtz et al., 2015). When cold plasma discharges are spatially confined to the microscale dimension, microplasma is formed, which has higher energy density and a non-equilibrium reaction environment and is uniquely advantageous in fabricated nanomaterials (Chiang et al., 2020). The commonly used plasma discharge schematics are shown in Fig. 2 .

The discharge medium for cold plasma is usually a gas such as argon, nitrogen, oxygen, hydrogen, or atmosphere (Pan et al., 2022). Cold plasma can treat gases (Das & Roy, 2020; Lin et al., 2015), liquids (Habib et al., 2022; Zhu, Cheng, Ma, & Sun, 2023), Zhu, Cheng, Ma, & Sun, 2023nd solids (Peng et al., 2019; Wei et al., 2014). Cold plasma treats gases in which atoms or molecules in the gas undergo excitation and ionization, resulting in the formation of many active species that, under certain conditions, are deposited on the surface of the substrate to form nanostructures or nanoparticles (Cheng et al., 2023b). This is often called plasma-enhanced chemical vapour deposition (PECVD), a low-temperature, effective, homogeneous technique for preparing silicon films and carbon nanotubes (Sun et al., 2022; Yi et al., 2021). Cold plasma treatment of liquids produces reactive particles that interact at the liquid surface in a process that may involve reactions including degradation, polymerization, oxidation, and reduction, and the produced nanomaterials require further collection and purification (Weerasinghe et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2015; Zhu, Cheng, Ma, & Sun, 2023). Cold plasma can also treat solid powders and thin films (Peng et al., 2019; Wei et al., 2014). This kind of technology treats solids to create nanostructures through etching and chemical reactions on solid surfaces. Cold plasma equipment is still being developed for large-scale industrial production. Flow-type plasma reactors, microplasma reaction arrays, and other equipment more suitable for large-scale production are becoming the trend (Lin et al., 2020). Factors affecting nanomaterial fabrication by cold plasma systems include the form of discharge, the state of the treatment precursor (gaseous, liquid, solid), the type of active particles, and so on. It is important to select the appropriate cold plasma system to fabricate the nanomaterials so that the properties and structures of the desired nanomaterials can be obtained more efficiently and accurately.

The inorganic nanomaterials discussed here mainly include metal nanoparticles, metal oxide nanoparticles, and other metal-based nanoparticles. Metal nanoparticles can be applied in food packaging to provide antimicrobial and anticorrosive functions and can also be used in food testing to rapidly identify contaminants in food through techniques like surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) and fluorescence (Kumar et al., 2021; Nikolic et al., 2021; Tong et al., 2023). Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs), zinc oxide (ZnO) nanoparticles, and titanium dioxide (TiO2) nanoparticles are common inorganic nanomaterials in the food safety field. Conventional methods for the fabrication of inorganic nanomaterials include solvothermal, sol-gel, and vapour phase deposition. These conventional nanomaterial fabrication methods usually require complex operational procedures with high energy consumption, uneven product distribution, and environmental pollution (Jamkhande et al., 2019). With the increasing awareness of environmental protection, people are paying more and more attention to effective and environmentally friendly methods of fabricating inorganic nanomaterials. Fabrication of inorganic nanomaterials via cold plasma avoids the extensive use of toxic reducing or oxidizing agents, dispersants, and stabilizers and is more efficient and environmentally friendly than conventional fabrication methods for metal and metal oxide nanoparticles.

The most common method for fabricating metal nanomaterials is wet chemical reduction, but it requires the addition of additional reducing and capping agents and many steps (Beyene et al., 2017). Concentration, reducing agent, temperature, and stabilizer are among the crucial factors contributing to metal nanoparticle formation. Cold plasma enables rapid metal nanoparticle fabrication without adding toxic reducing agents. Cold plasma contains reactive substances such as electrons, ions and free radicals (Esua et al., 2022d). These substances are highly energetic and can effectively provide electrons to reduce other substances. Electrons are usually provided in a more controlled and predictable manner compared with conventional chemical reductants. In addition, cold plasma operates at uniquely low temperatures and can be performed at ambient pressure. This contrasts with some conventional chemical reductants that require specific temperature, pressure or solvent conditions to function effectively. Cold plasma fabrication of Ag (Habib et al., 2022; Weerasinghe et al., 2022), Au (Cyganowski et al., 2019), Ni (Lin et al., 2018), Tungsten (Lee et al., 2014) and other metal nanoparticles has been studied. Cold plasma is used as a reducing agent to reduce metal ions to nanoparticles, typically using Ar and H2 as discharge gases. Argon atoms and a small amount of nitrogen produced by the argon DBD discharge create a complex chemical reaction at the plasma-liquid interface in which silver ions capture the solvated electrons and are reduced to silver atoms (Weerasinghe et al., 2020). In addition to colloidal metal nanoparticles dispersed in solution, cold plasma can also assist in the preparation of composite nanomaterials consisting of a combination of metal nanomaterials and a substrate, which includes CNTs, GO, g-C3N4, hydrogels, and others. Such composite nanomaterials may result in a homogeneous dispersion of metal nanoparticles and may be more adaptable to complex food safety application scenarios. For example, cold plasma fabricates highly dispersed GO-based AgNPs by dry reduction of Ag+ with better antimicrobial activity (Wei et al., 2014). Cyganowski et al. (2019) prepared 7-nm averaged-size AuNPs stabilized in a hydrogel by glow discharge. Without adding any additional reducing and capping agents, a variety of ROS, RNS, hydrogen radicals and electrons generated by glow discharge reduced gold ions to AuNPs and immobilized them in the liquid phase of hydrophilic monomers, which polymerized to obtain quaternary ammonium hydrogels loaded with AuNPs. Pt/g-C3N4 composite photocatalysts were fabricated by cold plasma reduction of H2PtCl6 on g-C3N4 (Ding et al., 2021). Ultrafine Pt nanoparticles were obtained by cold plasma reduction of H2PtCl6 and uniformly deposited on g-C3N4. Changing the discharge power of the plasma can effectively control the size of the Pt nanoparticles, the electronic structure, and the interaction between the Pt nanoparticles and g-C3N4.

Cold plasma can reduce metal ions to fabricate metal nanoparticles or oxidize metal ions to fabricate metal oxide nanoparticles. Some cold plasma fabricated metal oxide nanoparticles have been studied, including Ag2O (Yang et al., 2015), RuO2 (Ananth & Mok, 2014), ZnO (Liao et al., 2023), NiO (Zhang et al., 2023), TiO2 (Zhu et al., 2021) and others. For instance, O2 plasma can oxidize Ag colloids and induce the formation of Ag2O nanostructures, but H2 plasma treatment after O2 plasma treatment reduced Ag2O to Ag, and the plasma-induced nano-structural changes were irreversible (Yang et al., 2015). Ananth and Mok (2016) fabricated Ag2O, Ag2O/RuO2 nanomaterials by DBD plasma using AgNO3 and RuCl solutions as precursors. In addition to the bottom-up fabrication of metal oxide nanoparticles with metal ions as precursors, another class of strategies involves the top-down fabrication of metal oxide nanoparticles by cold plasma electrolysis with metal electrodes as precursors (see Fig. 3 ). Concentrated current heating increases the local temperature of the electrode, resulting in local melting and quenching of the molten electrode portion in the liquid phase for conversion to the target nanoparticles. The fabrication of TiO2 via Ti electrodes by Zhu et al. (2021) and ZnO nanoflowers via zinc wires by Saito et al. (2011) were based on this strategy.

Carbon nanomaterials, including carbon nanotubes (CNTs), carbon dots (CDs), and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) have multiple applications in food safety. They can be applied in food packaging materials to improve leakage prevention and freshness preservation and to increase the shelf life of fresh food (Raul et al., 2022), as biosensors to detect pollutants in food and to optimize the precision and sensitivity of food detection (Khan et al., 2023; Sonali et al., 2022); and in food processing to remove harmful substances, purify water, or carry out sterilization and disinfection to ensure food safety (Gusain et al., 2020). Conventional methods of fabricating carbon nanomaterials include chemical vapour deposition, arc discharge, laser ablation, and hydrothermal methods (Speranza, 2021). Cold plasma fabrication of carbon nanomaterials offers some unique advantages (see Fig. 4 ).

Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials with particle sizes typically less than 10 nm. There are two main methods for fabricating carbon dots: top-down and bottom-up. Cold plasma preparations of carbon dots mostly use a bottom-up strategy (Kurniawan et al., 2021; Mohammadzaheri et al., 2022; Pho et al., 2023; Weerasinghe et al., 2022; Yang, Pai, & Chiang, 2019) where small molecules are aggregated into nanoparticles. Compared to traditional hydrothermal methods, plasma preparation of carbon dots offers significant time savings. N-doped carbon dots were fabricated by hydrothermally treating folic acid and ethylene glycol mixed solution at 180 °C for 12 h (Zhang & Chen, 2014). Fabricating N-doped carbon dots by immersing a microplasma jet into a microporous-sized gas-liquid reactor containing an aqueous folic acid solution requires only 20–60 min of treatment (Pho et al., 2023). The former has a quantum yield of only 15.7%, whereas the latter has a quantum yield of 35%.

Hydroxyl radicals and solvated electrons generated by cold plasma and liquid reactions are involved in fabricating N-doped carbon dots and functionalizing their surfaces. The final N-doped carbon dots (NGQDs) have tunable emission wavelengths (Kurniawan et al., 2021). One study has revealed the formation of plasma material by optical emission spectroscopy, proposing a reaction mechanism for preparing carbon dots from folic acid as a carbon source (Pho et al., 2023).

Cold plasma can reduce graphene oxide (GO) to obtain reduced graphene oxide (rGO) at atmospheric pressure directly (Peng et al., 2019). Energetic electrons and ions generated by the cold plasma discharge collide on the GO surface, realizing energy transfer to the oxygen-containing functional groups on the GO surface. Excited oxygen groups quickly react to form CO2, CO, and steam, increasing pressure between GO nanosheets and causing partial reduction and exfoliation (Jiang et al., 2020). The plasma reduction of GO can also be accompanied by the doping of elements such as B (Li et al., 2016), N, and S (Miao et al., 2019). The doping of heteroatoms can cause defects that change the electronic structure of the rGO surface and improve the electrochemical and catalytic properties of rGO (Liu et al., 2011). Moreover, the plasma can realize the reduction of GO and metal ions at the same time to prepare metal nanoparticles or metal oxide-doped rGO in one step. Incorporating metal nanoparticles or metal oxides can improve the performance of materials. For example, AgNPs@rGO has excellent antimicrobial performance (Liu et al., 2021), and rGO/NiO has good electrochemical performance (Zhang et al., 2023).

In addition to CDs and rGO, cold plasma has demonstrated unique advantages in fabricating other carbon nanomaterials such as CNTs, porous carbons (PCs), and graphite phase carbon nitride (g-C3N4). The fabrication of multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) with controllable diameters in a mild environment by microwave plasma vapour deposition and catalyzed by Fe nanoparticles has been reported (Das & Roy, 2020). CNTs were also induced to grow by the catalysis of Ni and the degradation carbonization of PVP fibres with oxygen functional groups introduced through oxygen plasma discharge (Phan et al., 2023). The traditional method of g-C3N4 fabrication is high-temperature calcination over a long time and at a high temperature. To obtain g-C3N4, melamine, thiourea, and urea were heated in a crucible at an elevated rate of 5 °C/min to 550 °C for 2 h (Zhu et al., 2015). However, Li et al. (2018) yielded g-C3N4 using DBD plasma treatment of melamine for 15–45 min (H2 flow rate: 40 mL min−1, power supply: 50 V × 0.4 A). Compared to traditional roasting, plasma treatment increased sulfur doping in g-C3N4, leading to higher activity (Ma et al., 2018). Cold plasma has also shown advantages in the fabrication of porous carbons. Lin et al. (2015) introduced ethanol vapour and argon into a DBD plasma reactor, where PCs were formed directly on the surface of the metal electrodes during the discharge process. This method was one-step, template-free, and highly efficient. The PCs synthesized by this method had high specific surface area and high pore volume with excellent adsorption properties.

Nanoporous frameworks are polymers constructed from rigid molecules with a nanoporous structure. Diverse compositions, tunable structures, and large specific surface areas characterize nanoporous frameworks. As new nanoporous frameworks materials, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and covalent organic frameworks (COFs) have attracted much attention in food safety applications such as food contaminant detection and food packaging (Cheng et al., 2021; Huang, Feng, et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2019). Most MOFs and COFs are fabricated by solvothermal methods, which are cumbersome, time-consuming, and energy-intensive. The development of environmentally friendly, easy-to-handle, and efficient fabrication methods is in great demand.

MOFs are porous crystalline materials, network-structured crystals of metal ions or metal clusters coordinated with organic ligands (Safaei et al., 2019). Conventional MOF fabrication methods include diffusion, hydrothermal (solvothermal), microwave, and electrochemical methods. These methods have the disadvantages of high energy consumption, prolonged reaction time, and complicated operation. Cold plasma has successfully been used to prepare Fe-based MOFs (Huang, Chang, et al., 2023; Jin et al., 2021), Ce-MOF (Tang et al., 2022; Tao et al., 2019), Ln-MOF (Hou et al., 2022). In contrast, cold plasma preparation of MOFs is simple, energy efficient, fast, and can produce many active sites with different structures and morphologies (Karimzadeh et al., 2023).

For instance, Jiang et al. (2019) first proposed the fabrication of MOFs via DBD liquid phase plasma to establish a shortcut for electric field-driven deprotonation of linkers in cold plasma, rapid nucleation, and crystal growth of MOFs. In their studies of MOF preparation in DMF or alcohol, the DMF radical anion and electrons generated by DBD treatment can accelerate the deprotonation of organic ligands, leading to rapid MOF fabrication. Based on this preparation strategy, the team successfully fabricated classic MOFs including MOF-5, ZIF-8, and HKUST-1 (see Fig. 5
). Karimzadeh et al. (2023) selected Ni and Co as metal centres to prepare Ni–Co MOF nanocomposites by a one-step DBD plasma method using dicarboxylic acid (BDC) as ligand. Optical emission spectra (OES) were collected inside the discharge gas, at the gas-liquid interface, and inside the solution during the fabrication process, and the results showed that the electrons easily extracted hydrogen from the linker and that there were stabilized intermediates containing C–N bonds during the fabrication process. The results are consistent with Jiang et al. (2019).

COFs are periodically arranged organic polymers with a certain degree of crystallinity and high porosity, consisting of light elements (B, C, N, O, Si, H) linked by strong covalent bonds (Ding & Wang, 2013). Unlike conventional solvothermal fabrication methods, cold plasma enables rapid nucleation and growth of COFs under mild conditions, and the prepared COFs still have good crystallinity and stability. He et al. (2021) first rapidly prepared eight classical 2D COFs: COF-1, COF-5, COF-8, COF-10, ILCOF-1, Py-COF, NUS-2, and TpPa in less than 1 h using DBD liquid plasma (see Fig. 5). In addition, the cold plasma fabrication method can directly regulate the crystal modulation of COF-1 interlayer stacking through solvent selection rather than high-temperature activation. They attributed the rapid nucleation and growth of COFs under cold plasma conditions to the high electron temperature, abundance of reactive radicals, and acidic properties of the plasma. Based on the cold plasma fabricated COFs by He et al. (2021), Yuan et al. (2023) modified the COFs by β-cyclodextrin. The COFs provided continuous and closed open nanopore sizes, which provided high specific surface area interfaces for interactions between β-cyclodextrin and analytes. β-cyclodextrin-modified COFs can be a versatile chiral stationary phase for a wide range of enantiomeric separations in gas chromatography and high-performance liquid chromatography. It is shown that COFs fabricated by cold plasma-induced polymerization have promising applications in analytical detection. Until now, researches on the preparation of COFs by cold plasma fabrication have been limited, and the fabrication mechanism is still under investigation.

Biomass nanomaterials are nanoscale materials obtained by processing biomass such as wood, straw, and cellulose as raw materials. Biomass-derived nanomaterials including biochar and nanocellulose have applications in food packaging and adsorption of pollutants. Biochar is a carbonaceous material derived from the pyrolysis or oxidation of biomass (wood chips, straw, fruit shells). Biochar can improve soil, raise crop yields, and reduce the use of chemical fertilizers while reducing CO2 greenhouse gas emissions (Lehmann et al., 2021). Biochar can adsorb heavy metals and contaminants for environmental protection and purify water and air (Zhou et al., 2021). In addition, biochar has a wide range of applications in batteries, eco-construction, and other fields (Gupta et al., 2015). The particle size of biochar is typically in the micrometer range, which does not strictly meet the definition of a nanomaterial (Wang & Wang, 2019). However, Biochar may have nanostructures or nanofeatures, such as nanoscale pore structures or nanoscale features after surface functionalization, and is classified as a nanomaterial in this review. The temperature of the cold plasma does not decompose the biomass into Biochar, and the effect on the Biochar is mainly etching and introducing functional groups. Some related studies on cold plasma-activated Biochar for Hg removal have been reported. For example, chlorine plasma discharge introduced chlorine functional groups on the surface of Biochar (Wang et al., 2018), H2S plasma treatment increased C–S groups (Zhang et al., 2019), and HCl plasma introduced chlorine active sites in Biochar (Luo et al., 2019). The introduced functional groups significantly improved the adsorption performance of Hg.

The unique properties of nanocellulose, such as its large specific surface area, good mechanical properties, and biocompatibility, have aroused great interest. Cold plasma modification of cellulose has been extensively studied (Delaux et al., 2016; Samanta et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2022). On the one hand, the transient high-energy discharge of the cold plasma can disrupt the surface structure of the cellulose and break the hydrogen bonding network, and further, the reactive oxygen produced by the discharge can lead to rearrangement, isomerization, and cracking of the cellulose structure (Han, Zhu, & Cheng, 2022). Liquid phase plasma is an advanced oxidation method because it produces high-energy reactive particles such as hydroxyl radicals (Pradhan et al., 2022). From top to bottom, researchers have proposed different preparation strategies for cold plasma treatment of cellulose to prepare nanocellulose. Surov et al. (2018) proposed an oxidation-hydrolysis strategy in which the commercial microcrystalline cellulose (MCC) solution was first plasma-treated and then hydrolyzed with sulfuric acid. The cold plasma treatment caused the cellulose to oxidize and form carboxyl groups on the surface. The results showed that the release of cellulose nanocrystalline (CNC) particles was facilitated by this oxidation-hydrolysis strategy, and the yield of CNC was doubled up to 56%. In contrast, Zhu, Cheng, Ma, and Sun (2023) proposed different oxidation strategies. They proposed Fe2+ pretreatment of pineapple peel cellulose followed by cold plasma treatment, in which the combination of cold plasma and Fenton reaction achieved efficient oxidation and prepared cellulose nanofibrils.

A two-phase air plasma-activated/mild alkaline (TPAP/MA) pretreatment method was reported for the preparation of nanofibrillated cellulose (NFC) functionalized with free terminal carboxylic and amino-decorated surfaces from wheat straw (Shaghaleh et al., 2021). The researchers calculated the yield and energy requirements for the large-scale production of NFCs and found that the energy consumption of TPAP/MA pretreatment was reduced by 65.06–94.11% depending on the degree of activation, and the yield of NFCs was increased by 12.5–26.4%, which satisfied the principles of green chemistry (Shaghaleh et al., 2021). This study demonstrated that cold plasma had a very high potential for application in assisting large-scale industrial fabrication of CNFs.

Antimicrobial materials play an essential role in food safety (Pan et al., 2020). Antimicrobial materials can be used as antimicrobial coatings on food processing equipment to reduce the frequency of cleaning and sanitizing during processing, increasing yields and reducing costs (Ali et al., 2021a). Antimicrobial materials can inhibit the growth of microorganisms during food packaging and transportation, keeping food fresh and extending its shelf life. Common antimicrobial nanomaterials include metal and metal oxide nanoparticles, such as AgNPs and ZnO nanoparticles, and carbon nanoparticles, such as CNTs, CDs, and rGO (Ali et al., 2022).

Common metal and metal oxide nanoparticles can be used as antimicrobial materials. An essential mechanism of antimicrobial importance for metals and metal oxides is the forming of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which leads to subsequent oxidative stress in tissues (Fu et al., 2014). AgNPs are the most commonly used nanomaterials as antimicrobial agents in the food industry. Several studies have shown that the smaller the particle size of AgNPs, the stronger the antimicrobial properties (Dzimitrowicz et al., 2018; Weerasinghe et al., 2020). Dzimitrowicz et al. (2018) fabricated AgNPs with a size distribution of 14.9 ± 7 nm and a minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of 1.64 μg/mL against Erwinia amylovora and Clavibacter michiganensis by glow discharge. It was also reported that Weerasinghe et al. (2020) successfully fabricated AgNPs with a size distribution of 9.99 ± 3.4 nm using DBD solution plasma treatment for 10 min. DBD plasma fabricated Ag–TiO2 nanocomposites with AgNPs having an average particle size of 3.7 nm and uniformly dispersed on TiO2. The nanocomposites showed low MICs of 1 mg/mL and 2 mg/L against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus, respectively (Dong et al., 2019). It has been shown that cold plasma is advantageous in the fabrication of small and dimensionally uniform AgNPs with excellent antimicrobial properties.

ZnO nanoparticles are also an essential antimicrobial agent. Ananth et al. (2017) prepared zinc oxide nanomaterials (ZnONMs) as a soft-jet plasma source. The modulation of the morphology of ZnONMs, including flakes, rods, and flowers, can be achieved by adjusting the concentration of precursors. The plasma-fabricated flower-like ZnONMs showed the best antimicrobial properties with MICs of 3.125 μg/mL and 0.7813 μg/mL against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus, respectively.

Carbon nanoparticles such as CDs, CNTs, rGO, and other carbon nanomaterials have relatively better biocompatibility as antimicrobial agents than inorganic antimicrobial nanomaterials such as metal and metal oxide nanoparticles (Raul et al., 2022). The antimicrobial mechanisms of carbon nanomaterials are relevant to their physics and chemistry properties, of which the most commonly observed is physical and mechanical damage to the bacterial outer membrane or cell wall (Xin et al., 2019). Another important mechanism of carbon nanomaterial antimicrobial activity is the generation of ROS by oxidative stress, which inactivates proteins and destroys bacterial nucleic acids, thus acting as an antimicrobial agent (Esua et al., 2022a). A study has fabricated multifunctional carbon dots using polydiethanol as a precursor and oxygen and nitrogen plasma discharge, which can effectively inhibit the growth of Escherichia coli and Acinetobacter baumannii (Park et al., 2017). Ke, Lu, and Chen (2018) reduced GO using inductively coupled RF plasma, and the cold plasma caused the decomposition of the GO lamellae into smaller layers with rougher and more defective surfaces, which were more damaging to the bacterial film and exhibited more muscular bactericidal activity against Escherichia coli. The results showed that plasma-treated GO concentrations as low as 0.02 mg/mL still exhibited potent antimicrobial activity (Ke, Ma, et al., 2018).

A synergistic antimicrobial effect can be observed when inorganic nanomaterials are combined with carbon nanomaterials. Cold plasma can reduce silver ions in situ to prepare uniformly dispersed AgNPs in nanosheet layer structures like CNTs or GO. Cold plasma dry reduction of silver ions to prepare AgNPs highly dispersed on graphene oxide with diameters around 2 nm (Wei et al., 2014). For example, Yan et al. (2016) prepared AgNPs functionalized carbon nanotube carriers by electron reduction of silver ions generated by RF plasma. Both of these papers reported excellent antimicrobial activity of the prepared nanomaterials. On the one hand, the two-dimensional lamellar structure of CNTs and GO as a support can make AgNPs uniformly dispersed and reduce agglomeration to realize the controlled release of silver ions. On the other hand, cold plasma-fabricated AgNPs in carbon nanomaterials in situ with significantly smaller particle size, and at a certain amount of Ag, smaller AgNPs resulted in the participation of more AgNPs in the antimicrobial process, which is conducive to the enhancement of antimicrobial activity (Xin et al., 2019).

Although silver nanoparticles have sound antibacterial effects, their cytotoxicity should not be ignored (Esua et al., 2022b). Therefore, covering AgNPs with a biocompatible matrix reduces the health risks of free AgNPs. Chitosan, a natural polymer with good biocompatibility and biodegradability, can have a synergistic antimicrobial effect when combined with AgNPs. Chitosan nanofibers prepared by electrostatic spinning through RF plasma treatment were reported to achieve the fabrication and immobilization of AgNPs on the surface. The plasma etching effect resulted in the maximal exposure of the AgNPs, which had excellent antibacterial activity (Annur et al., 2015). Sun et al. (2020) also prepared AgNPs/chitosan nanocomposites for antimicrobial applications in situ via microplasma. During the dissolution process of nanocomposite films, ROS including superoxide anion (
•
O 2 −
) and H2O2 formed in AgNPs/chitosan films can diffuse or migrate to the surface and destabilize the plasma membrane potential upon contact with bacteria (Sun et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection and removal of pollutant residues
ce:section.ce:para: Detection and removal of pollutant residues is critical in food safety. Pollutant residues include heavy metals, veterinary drugs, pesticides, illegal additives, and other contaminations, potentially harming human health (Pan et al., 2021). Detecting the presence and concentration of contaminants can detect hidden food safety hazards promptly to protect consumers’ rights and interests. At the same time, effective decontamination measures can reduce the content of harmful substances in food, purify food, and improve food quality and safety (Cheng et al., 2023a). Regular monitoring and removal of contaminants not only helps to ensure that food complies with hygiene standards but also helps to reduce the incidence of food poisoning and safeguard public health. Some studies have been carried out on using plasma to prepare nanomaterials to detect and remove these pollutant residues.

Metal ion contamination in food can severely affect human health, as can lead, mercury, and other heavy metals, which can cause poisoning, and long-term intake can cause chronic diseases (Esua et al., 2022c). Therefore, metal ion testing can detect metal contamination on time to ensure food safety. Metal ion testing also helps monitor the source of contamination in the production process, improve the quality management level of production, and prevent metal contamination in food.

Instead of a reducing agent, cold plasma can fabricate high-purity AuNPs rapidly in a short period. Therefore, Li et al. (2022) proposed the instant fabrication of glucose-functionalized AuNPs (G-AuNPs) via a microfluidic plasma system to achieve colourimetric sensing detection of Pb2+ in 30 s. Compared to conventional chromatographic or spectroscopic-based detection techniques for Pb2+, this assay is simple, rapid, and has great potential for real-time in situ sensing. MOFs have adjustable pore size, adsorption, and easy functionalization, which can promote interaction with the detected substances and convert the interaction into detectable luminescent signals (Cheng et al., 2021). MOFs combine adsorption properties and luminescent response, which is conducive to detection sensitivity (Cheng et al., 2021). The luminescent metal Eu3+ as the centre is one of the luminescence strategies of MOF. Lanthanide metal-organic frameworks Eu(BTB) MOFs fabricated by DBD plasma at room temperature for 20 min were used for the trace detection of Fe3+, which was rapid and sensitive with the limit of detection of 0.5 μM (Hou et al., 2022). The fluorescence quenching mechanism of carbon dots is also widely used for metal ion detection (Qu et al., 2018). Metal ions reported to be detected in cold plasma-prepared carbon dots include Hg2+ (Ke, Lu, & Chen, 2018), Fe3+ (Treepet et al., 2022), and uranium (VI) (Wang et al., 2015).

Carbon nanomaterials and Biochar are widely used as adsorbents due to their large specific surface area, porous structure, ease of modification, and chemical stability (Gusain et al., 2020). The cold plasma-treated Biochar has a larger BET surface area and pore volume, and the introduction of high-density oxygen functional groups enhances the hydrophilicity and metal affinity, especially the maximum adsorption capacity of Pb2+ increased by 40% (Zhou et al., 2022). GO was prepared from graphite rods of recycled batteries using solution plasma stripping. The fabricated GO had a large surface area and high porosity (Van Hao et al., 2020). Pb2+ was removed by interacting with the π-π* bonding electrons of GO and oxygen-containing functional groups. Cold plasma-prepared nanomaterials composite of reduced graphene oxide and metal nanoparticles (e.g., nanoscale zero-valent iron particles supported on rGO (NZVI/rGOs), rGO-Ag) have also been used for the adsorption of heavy metals (Han, Li, et al., 2022; Li et al., 2015). The results showed that the metal ion removal efficiency of NZVI/rGOs was higher than that of NZVI particles and rGOs alone, which was attributed to the synergistic effect of reduction of NZVI particles and adsorption of rGOs (Li et al., 2015).

Drug substances enter water bodies and contaminate the environment, posing a significant threat to food safety (Wu et al., 2022). Antibiotics such as tetracycline, avermectin, gentamicin, and oxytetracycline are widely used for animal growth and treatment, which may lead to drug residues in meat or dairy products, as well as the potential for contamination of water bodies and adverse effects on the human body such as allergies and antimicrobial resistance (Lebelo et al., 2021; Esua et al., 2021b). For instance, Skіba et al. (2023) achieved photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline by depositing AgNPs onto TiO2 powder via plasma discharge reduction, and the photocatalytic efficiency of TiO2 deposited with AgNPs was higher than that of pure TiO2. Also, Ke et al. (2019) prepared carbon dots by capacitively coupled plasma discharge using citrate ion solution as a precursor, and the limit of detection of norfloxacin by proportional fluorescence was 7.3 nM.

4-Nitrophenol is an essential compound in chemicals and pharmaceuticals, and its contamination in the environment can pose a threat to human health. Therefore, reducing 4-nitrophenol in the climate is crucial to non-toxic 4-aminophenol. AuNPs fabricated by glow discharge were immobilized on [2-(acryloyloxy) ethyl] trimethylammonium chloride, which was subsequently crosslinked by N-methylenebis (acrylamide). The resulting hydrogel-based nanocomposites are suitable for reducing 4-nitrophenol (Cyganowski et al., 2019). AuNPs/rGO nanocomposites prepared by one-step double reduction of GO and HAuCl in cold plasma were also reported to be used for the reduction of 4-nitrophenol with reduction rate constants up to 0.422 min−1 (Yang, Yu, et al., 2019).

Nanomaterials can enhance the physical properties of food packaging, extend the freshness of food, resist bacteria and mould, and improve the transparency and strength of packaging while reducing packaging weight and resource consumption (Wu et al., 2022). For example, AgNPs can inhibit bacterial growth, and nano-silica can improve the barrier performance of packaging against oxygen and water vapour (Socas-Rodríguez et al., 2017). Nanomaterials can also be used in innovative packaging to monitor food freshness and safety.

Nanomaterials can be used as fillers in food packaging to improve mechanical, barrier, and antimicrobial properties (Silvestre et al., 2011). Metal and metal oxide nanoparticles are added to polymers for food packaging. Polylactic acid (PLA) is a biodegradable, naturally renewable polymer used broadly in food packaging. However, single PLA's low thermal stability and poor mechanical properties limit further applications. Therefore, nanoparticles are doped into the polymer as fillers to improve its mechanical properties and antimicrobial activity. Sirotkin et al. (2021) prepared AgNPs and formed polymer nanocomposite PLA/AgNPs films by silver electrode plasma discharge in poly (propylene glycol) acid suspension. The films showed vigorous antimicrobial activity against bacteria and yeasts. A single filler may not be able to meet the multifaceted performance enhancements of PLA at the same time. Multicomponent nanoparticles containing carbon, tungsten carbide (WC) and silver were prepared in one step by tungsten electrode plasma discharge (Boonyeun et al., 2021). The results showed that incorporating the multicomponent nanoparticles filler increased Young's modulus and tensile strength of the PLA films, and the crystallinity was about 10 times that of the pure PLA films. AgNPs in the composite films allowed them to exhibit antimicrobial activity against typical bacterial Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus (Boonyeun et al., 2021).

Real-time monitoring is achieved through nanosensors that detect changes in gas composition, humidity, pH, and volatiles during the transportation of food packaging. The rGO prepared by hydrogen plasma reduction of GO was spin-coated on pre-patterned silicon to obtain a carbon dioxide gas sensor (Muhammad Hafiz et al., 2014). This CO2 gas sensor had good repeatability with a maximum CO2 response of 15% in an air environment (68% RH) and required no external aid for recovery. Ethylene is an essential marker of ripening in respiratory leapfrog fruits and is vital to monitor in real time during food packaging and transportation. Barreca et al. (2018) fabricated fluorine-doped β-MnO2 nanoparticles by plasma-assisted vapour deposition. The nanoparticles were fabricating a chemo-resistive gas sensor, which showed good response and selectivity to ethylene.

It has been confirmed that cold plasma holds great promise for fabricating nanomaterials for food safety. Some limitations and challenges also exist. Here are some future perspectives on cold plasma-fabricated nanomaterials for food safety applications.
•
Deeper understanding of cold plasma nanomaterial fabrication mechanisms. Cold plasma mainly fabricates inorganic nanomaterials such as metals and metal oxides. At the same time, little research has been done to fabricate emerging carbon nanomaterials, framework nanomaterials, and biomass nanomaterials. There is also no unified theory to explain the mechanism of cold plasma fabrication of nanomaterials. A more in-depth study of the mechanism of cold plasma fabrication of nanomaterials is highly desirable.
•
Development of equipment for cold plasma fabrication of nanomaterials. Cold plasma devices for research and development of fabricating nanomaterials are important because the cold discharge form and discharge parameters can directly affect the structure, morphology and properties of the fabricated nanomaterials. There is a need to develop equipment more suitable for cold plasma to fabricate nanomaterials to achieve precise control of the synthesis conditions and thus prepare high quality nanomaterials.
•
Expanding the scope of cold plasma-fabricated nanomaterials for applications in the food safety field. There is still limited research on fabricating nanomaterials using cold plasma for food safety applications, with most focusing on the antimicrobial function of metal nanomaterials. Nanomaterials fabricated by cold plasma can be used for surface coating of food packaging materials to improve packaging materials' antimicrobial and anticorrosive properties. Cold plasma-fabricated nanomaterials can also be used to detect and adsorb harmful substances in food and improve the sensitivity and accuracy of food detection. Therefore, the advantages of cold plasma-fabricated nanomaterials should be utilized to expand its application in food safety.
•
Safety assessment of nanomaterials fabricated by cold plasma. This paper reviews the application of cold plasma-fabricated nanomaterials in ensuring food safety, but cold plasma is an innovative strategy for fabricating nanomaterials, and it is necessary to assess the safety of cold plasma-fabricated nanomaterials themselves. While cold plasma-fabricated nanomaterials may have excellent properties, their safety needs to be thoroughly investigated. This includes a comprehensive study and testing of the toxicity, mobility and stability of nanomaterials fabricated by cold plasma.
•
Promoting large-scale and low-cost fabrication of nanomaterials through cold plasma. Cold plasma requires less energy than techniques such as chemical vapour deposition, mechanical methods, and laser pyrolysis, which require high temperatures and high energy output and can significantly reduce energy costs. With the solution gel method, cold plasma fabrication of nanomaterials can reduce the consumption of chemical reagents. Cold plasma equipment has a relatively simple structure and low operating costs, making it easy to achieve large-scale production. Cold plasma has the advantages of saving energy consumption, green environment, and high efficiency in scaling up production. However, the fabrication of cold plasmonic nanomaterials is still being carried out on a small scale in the laboratory. Scaling up production on an industrial scale requires developing equipment such as continuous-flow cold plasma reactors or microplasma reaction arrays.

In contrast to conventional methods for fabricating nanomaterials, cold plasma preparation presents notable advantages in efficiency, high purity, and controllability. Cold plasma fabrication is environmentally friendly, producing minimal waste and reducing the need for harsh chemicals, aligning with sustainable practices in the food industry. Cold plasma-fabricated nanomaterials have applications in antimicrobial, detection, and removal of pollutant residues, food packaging, and other food safety applications. Future studies should further explore the methodology, safety evaluation, and application of cold plasma preparation of nanomaterials to promote their practical application in the field of food safety. The advantages of cold plasma-fabricated nanomaterials should be utilized to expand their application in food safety. It is hoped that this review will attract more attention and convince people that cold plasma-fabricated nanomaterials have promising applications in ensuring food safety.

This research was also supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (2023YFD2101000, 2023YFD2101002), the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2024A1515011498), TCL Young Scholar Foundation, the Guangdong International Science and Technology Cooperation Base of Contemporary Food Engineering (2019A050519001), and the Guangzhou Key Laboratory for Intelligent Sensing and Quality Control of Agricultural Products (202102100009).
